narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Thursday, May 7, 2026
First Warn Weather Headlines:
A steady warming trend begins today with highs climbing into the upper 70s and lower 80s by Saturday.
Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms are expected Friday through Sunday.
A few stronger storms Friday afternoon and evening could produce hail, mainly across central and southeast Missouri.
A stronger cold front arrives late this weekend, bringing cooler temperatures back Sunday into early next week.
Additional scattered rain chances may linger into next week, but confidence increases in another warming trend by midweek.

After several cooler-than-normal days across Missouri and Illinois, a much more active and warmer spring weather pattern is beginning to unfold across the region. Sunshine will dominate much of the day today as southwest winds increase behind this morning’s chilly start. Those southwest winds are important because they are ushering warmer air back into the Mid-Mississippi Valley, helping temperatures rebound into the 60s this afternoon. While that may still feel somewhat cool compared to where we should be climatologically in May, it marks the beginning of a significant warm-up heading into the weekend.

Tonight will remain quiet with partly cloudy skies and seasonably cool overnight temperatures. By Friday morning, however, the weather pattern begins transitioning toward a more unsettled setup as a weak cold front slowly drops south into the region. Increasing moisture streaming northward from the southwest combined with several upper-level disturbances moving overhead will provide the ingredients necessary for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop through the day.

The morning hours Friday may feature isolated showers, especially north of Interstate 70, although dry air near the surface may initially limit rainfall totals. By Friday afternoon and evening, moisture levels will continue increasing while instability develops across portions of central and southeast Missouri. Atmospheric instability values are not particularly impressive for early May, but they will be sufficient for scattered thunderstorms to develop along and south of the frontal boundary.

The overall severe weather setup Friday is not especially concerning, and widespread severe storms are not expected. However, a few isolated stronger thunderstorms cannot be completely ruled out during the afternoon and evening hours. If any storms manage to briefly intensify, hail would likely be the primary threat given cooler temperatures aloft and modest instability. Wind shear values will be somewhat supportive of organized convection, but the atmosphere overall appears rather “skinny” and stretched vertically, which is not typically favorable for sustained severe thunderstorm development. Because of that, the severe weather risk remains low at this time.

Rain and thunderstorm activity Friday night will gradually shift southeast and should largely exit the area before midnight. Behind Friday’s front, little to no cooler air actually follows, meaning the warm-up continues uninterrupted into Saturday. In fact, Saturday is shaping up to be the warmest day many areas have experienced in several weeks.

Temperatures Saturday afternoon are forecast to surge into the upper 70s and lower 80s across much of Missouri and Illinois as warm air advection intensifies ahead of another approaching cold front. With intervals of sunshine and breezy southerly winds, it will feel much more like late spring across the region. If you have outdoor plans Saturday, much of the daytime period appears favorable before rain chances increase again late Saturday night.

A much stronger cold front is forecast to sweep through the region late Saturday night into Sunday. This system will bring another round of showers and a few thunderstorms, although the timing currently appears less favorable for severe weather. Forecast guidance suggests instability levels will remain fairly weak, with only modest elevated instability available ahead of the front. In addition, a stronger capping inversion may limit thunderstorm redevelopment during the day Sunday.

What does appear increasingly likely is a return to cooler conditions Sunday into early next week. Forecast confidence remains somewhat lower regarding exact temperatures because much depends on the timing of the frontal passage and how quickly cooler air filters into the region. However, if clouds and cold air advection linger throughout Sunday, some areas may struggle to warm much at all during the afternoon.

Looking ahead into next week, additional weak disturbances may bring occasional rain chances, although forecast models continue to differ considerably regarding exact timing and coverage. One consistent signal among the guidance is the expectation for another gradual warming trend by the middle part of next week as the broader pattern becomes less amplified.

For now, enjoy today’s sunshine because the next several days will feature a little bit of everything that springtime in Missouri and Illinois is famous for: warming temperatures, scattered thunderstorms, brief humidity, and another cooldown waiting right around the corner.
